label: SES-064 V2 remote-access deployment architecture decided (rebased from SES-062 to SES-064 to clear collisions with PI-025 prior-conversations backfill planning sandbox (SES-062) and PI-029 commit entity schema spec sandbox (SES-063)): local-canonical DB stays on Doug's laptop, MCP exposed publicly via Cloudflare Tunnel under crmbuilder.com with Cloudflare Access + shared-secret auth, REST API stays on localhost, marker-driven engagement routing. Transport verification against the codebase confirms the current MCP server runs stdio-only (server.py:44 — server.run('stdio')) and needs an HTTP transport adapter (FastMCP supports streamable-http). 1 session, 4 decisions, 1 planning item, 5 references.

session.title: V2 remote-access deployment architecture decided: Option A (local-canonical DB + tunneled MCP) approved; transport verified stdio-only; marker-driven engagement routing chosen; tunnel under crmbuilder.com via Cloudflare Tunnel + Access
session.conversation_reference: Claude.ai conversation opened against the seed prompt 'We need to deploy the CRM Builder V2 api and mcp somewhere where claude.ai can begin accessing the information stored in the database. There are way too many problems where it does not know what is current, or writes prompts that are out of date vs the database.' The conversation ran in ARCHITECTURE mode (project default) and worked through one consequential architectural decision plus three decide-and-announce follow-on decisions. The architectural decision (DEC-201) was framed using the eight-element consequential decision template with three options: (A) local-canonical database with public MCP via tunnel, (B) remote-canonical database on VPS or managed store with desktop UI and Claude.ai both as remote clients, (C) hybrid with stale read-only snapshot pushed to cloud. Option C was dismissed in the framing as not solving the stated problem (claude.ai writing out-of-date prompts because the snapshot it reads is also out of date). Doug approved Option A with the terse acknowledgment 'a'. After approval, the conversation verified the MCP transport against the codebase: cloned the crmbuilder repo with sparse checkout (CLAUDE.md, PRDs/, crmbuilder-v2/), read crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/mcp_server/server.py line 44 to confirm server.run('stdio') and the docstring 'Claude Desktop pipes here', counted 44 tools exposed in tools.py (broad read+write surface including replace_charter, create_decision, delete_reference, etc.), verified mcp>=1.0 dependency (FastMCP supports stdio/sse/streamable-http transports), and checked config.py confirming api_host='127.0.0.1' default. Three decide-and-announce decisions followed: DEC-202 (expose MCP server only, REST API stays localhost — narrows attack surface and matches Doug's single-laptop Claude Code workflow); DEC-203 (single remote MCP with marker-driven engagement routing — matches existing single-engagement-active workflow versus per-engagement tunnel deploys); DEC-204 (Cloudflare Tunnel on subdomain mcp.crmbuilder.com with Cloudflare Access identity gating — requires moving crmbuilder.com nameservers to Cloudflare from current registrar). Doug confirmed the domain selection unprompted ('We have the crmbuilder.com domain registered and it would make more sense to use that. It is not on cloudflair.'). No code changes authored in this conversation; the deliverables are the four governance decisions, the planning item PI-045 that scopes the implementation workstream, and the in-flight follow-on to draft the deployment kickoff prompt in a separate Claude.ai conversation. On push, the payload collided with a parallel sandbox that had pushed ses_062.json first (PI-025 prior-conversations backfill planning); this conversation rebased to SES-064 + DEC-201..201 + PI-045 and re-pushed.
session.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "We need to deploy the CRM Builder V2 api and mcp somewhere where claude.ai can begin accessing the information stored in the database. There are way too many problems where it does not know what is current, or writes prompts that are out of date vs the database."

Structured summary of the architectural decision conversation:

(0) Identifier rebase (two collisions). The close-out payload was originally drafted as ses_062.json claiming SES-062 + DEC-191..194 + PI-045 against snapshot heads of SES-061/DEC-190/PI-044. On first push, a parallel sandbox had pushed ses_062.json first (PI-025 prior-conversations backfill planning, DEC-191..197). Rebased to SES-063 + DEC-198..201 + PI-045. On second push, yet another parallel sandbox had pushed ses_063.json first (commit entity schema spec for PI-029, DEC-198..200). Rebased to SES-064 + DEC-201..204 + PI-045 (PIs head unchanged through both rebases because neither colliding payload authored planning items). Predecessor apply chain extended by two: SES-058 → SES-059 → SES-060 → SES-061 → SES-062 (PI-025 backfill planning, parallel sandbox A) → SES-063 (commit entity schema spec, parallel sandbox B) → SES-064 (this conversation). This is the same parallel-sandbox identifier-collision case the Code Change Lifecycle methodology conversation hit twice in SES-061 — three active sandboxes claiming consecutive identifiers within minutes strongly reinforces SES-061's flag that reserve-at-apply-time is the only collision-free model. The PI-032 methodology rollout scope should treat this as a near-term priority rather than a future-tense follow-on.

(1) Problem reframed. The pain Doug named — claude.ai writing prompts that are out of date versus the database — is the inverse of the v2 architectural premise. The database is supposed to be canonical for sessions, decisions, planning items, references; if claude.ai cannot read it, then claude.ai is not operating against canonical state and necessarily produces drift between conversation-memory inferences and reality. The fix is to give claude.ai a read+write path to the canonical store.

(2) Three options framed under the eight-element template. Option A: local-canonical DB with tunneled MCP — laptop stays source of truth, MCP server gets a public URL via tunnel, REST API stays on localhost, claude.ai reaches the MCP whenever the laptop is on and reachable. Option B: remote-canonical DB — move SQLite to a small VPS or migrate to managed DB (Postgres/Turso); desktop UI and claude.ai are both clients of the remote API; always-on but requires desktop UI rework for network latency and an offline story that does not exist today. Option C: hybrid scheduled push of read-only snapshot to cloud — dismissed as not solving the stated problem since stale snapshots produce the exact failure mode Doug is escaping. Why this matters: the v2 premise depends on every actor (desktop, claude.ai, Claude Code) reading and writing the same canonical store; today only two of three can reach it. The choice between A and B determines whether the gap closes by letting claude.ai reach into the laptop, or by moving the canonical store to a place all three actors reach equally. Recommendation: Option A. Cost named honestly: claude.ai data access is bounded by laptop online time; if Doug wants to think through a CBM question on a phone while the laptop is shut, claude.ai gets nothing. For the actual usage pattern (session work in front of the laptop) this is invisible; for ambient queries it is broken. Doug confirmed Option A with 'a'.

(3) Transport verification (post-approval prerequisite). Cloned dbower44022/crmbuilder via sparse checkout (CLAUDE.md, PRDs/, crmbuilder-v2/) at HEAD commit 8215614. Read crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/mcp_server/server.py — line 44 calls server.run('stdio'); the module docstring explicitly notes 'crmbuilder-v2-mcp # talk MCP over stdio (Claude Desktop pipes here)'. The current MCP server is stdio-only and not remotely reachable by claude.ai. Dependency check on pyproject.toml confirms mcp>=1.0 (the official Python SDK whose FastMCP class supports three transports — stdio, sse, streamable-http). Switching is a one-line change at the server.run() call site plus a CLI flag in cli.py:run_mcp. The REST API at config.py:55 binds to api_host='127.0.0.1' by default and stays unchanged — the MCP server speaks to it over localhost. Tool surface counted at 44 tools in tools.py including charter/status/decisions/sessions/risks/planning/topics/references CRUD plus catalog queries; the write surface includes replace_charter, create_decision, update_decision, delete_decision, create_session, replace_status, add_reference, delete_reference, create_risk, update_risk, create_planning_item, update_planning_item, create_topic, update_topic. The auth posture has to assume any caller could rewrite the charter, so Cloudflare Access identity gating at the edge plus a shared-secret header validated by the MCP HTTP transport is the right belt-and-braces pattern.

(4) Complication surfaced: engagement routing. The API is single-engagement per process — cli.py:run_api reads current_engagement.json (written by the desktop UI's Engagements panel) or --engagement flag at launch to pick which database to serve. CBM-engagement work and CRMBUILDER-engagement work read different databases. claude.ai sessions on either workstream need to reach the right one. Two viable options: (X) marker-driven, single remote MCP that follows whichever engagement is currently active in current_engagement.json; (Y) per-engagement deploys — two tunnels, two API+MCP process pairs, two claude.ai MCP URLs. (Y) is architecturally cleaner since claude.ai picks engagement by URL but roughly doubles the operational footprint. (X) matches the existing workflow with zero new state for Doug to manage. DEC-203 chose (X) decide-and-announce. Known footgun named: if Doug switches engagements mid-conversation in the desktop UI, the conversation's claude.ai reads switch silently to the new engagement; the API needs either to detect marker changes on each request or restart on marker change. Mitigation is part of PI-045's scope and is named in the kickoff prompt for the follow-on conversation.

(5) Public surface scope. DEC-202 decide-and-announce: expose the MCP server only via the tunnel, not the REST API. The MCP process talks to the API over localhost unchanged. Rationale: narrows the public attack surface to the MCP protocol; the 44 tools are the contract claude.ai consumes; REST endpoints would be redundant for claude.ai and double the auth surface. The alternative — exposing both — would let Claude Code prompts run remotely too (cross-machine appeal), but Doug runs Claude Code only at the laptop today; the marginal value does not justify the extra surface.

(6) Tunnel product and host. DEC-204 decide-and-announce: Cloudflare Tunnel on subdomain mcp.crmbuilder.com with Cloudflare Access policy gating by identity (Google login or similar) before requests reach the MCP, plus a shared-secret header the MCP validates. Doug confirmed crmbuilder.com is the right host (clearer than dougbower.com for project-scoped infrastructure) and noted it is currently not on Cloudflare — moving nameservers to Cloudflare (free plan, ~15 minutes) is a prerequisite operational step. Alternatives considered: Tailscale Funnel (no DNS migration but ties auth to tailnet membership rather than identity provider); ngrok (free tier unstable hostnames; paid ~$8/mo); raw SSH reverse tunnel (no identity gating). Cloudflare wins on stable hostname + free Access + project-scoped DNS.

(7) Planning item authored. PI-045 V2 remote-access deployment scopes the full workstream: (a) code changes — add --transport {stdio,streamable-http} flag to crmbuilder-v2-mcp CLI, parameterize transport in server.py main(), add shared-secret header middleware to the FastMCP HTTP transport, address the marker-change-mid-process risk identified in DEC-203 (re-read current_engagement.json per request or fail-loud on marker change with restart); (b) operational — move crmbuilder.com nameservers to Cloudflare, install cloudflared as a launchd service on Doug's laptop, create the named tunnel and Access policy for mcp.crmbuilder.com, register the MCP URL in claude.ai's MCP integration; (c) verification — smoke-test all 44 tools end-to-end, confirm auth gating blocks unauthenticated requests at both layers, confirm engagement marker drives the right database; (d) documentation — README section in crmbuilder-v2 covering remote deployment, entry in repo CLAUDE.md, kickoff prompt at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ that opens the implementation conversation. PI-045 status Open at end of this conversation.

(8) In-flight at end. The deployment kickoff prompt that PI-045 will be opened against is NOT authored in this conversation. The kickoff prompt is the deliverable of a follow-on Claude.ai conversation whose seed prompt will be 'draft the V2 remote-access deployment kickoff prompt for PI-045 per the four decisions in SES-064.' This split keeps the architectural decision conversation focused on its purpose and lets the kickoff drafting conversation read the four DECs as inputs.

(9) Sandbox commit and push. The ses_064.json close-out payload and CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-062.md apply prompt are committed and pushed together in one commit per the sandbox push convention.
session.artifacts_produced: Two governance artifacts pushed in the same commit: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_064.json (this payload) and PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-062.md (the apply prompt for Doug to run via Claude Code).
session.in_flight_at_end: Drafting the V2 remote-access deployment kickoff prompt for PI-045 in a separate follow-on Claude.ai conversation. The kickoff prompt sequences: (1) Claude Code prompt for the transport flag and shared-secret middleware in the MCP server; (2) Claude Code prompt addressing the engagement-marker-mid-process risk identified in DEC-203; (3) operational checklist for the Cloudflare nameserver migration, cloudflared launchd service install, named tunnel + Access policy creation, claude.ai MCP URL registration; (4) smoke-test plan for the 44-tool surface; (5) documentation updates to crmbuilder-v2 README and repo CLAUDE.md. The kickoff drafting conversation should open with the four decisions DEC-201..DEC-204 as inputs and produce the kickoff document at a path under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/.

decisions.title: V2 remote-access deployment architecture: local-canonical SQLite on Doug's laptop with public MCP via tunnel (Option A) versus remote-canonical cloud migration (Option B)
decisions.context: The v2 architectural premise is that a structured database is canonical for all governance artifacts (sessions, decisions, planning items, references, charter, status, risks, topics). Today only the desktop UI and Claude Code reach this database — both run on Doug's laptop against the local SQLite file. Claude.ai cannot reach it, so claude.ai conversations infer state from conversation memory and produce prompts and references that drift from canonical truth. The architectural fork is whether to close the gap by exposing the local database to claude.ai (keeping the laptop as source of truth) or by moving the canonical database to a cloud-hosted store that all three actors reach equally.
decisions.decision: Option A — local-canonical SQLite stays on Doug's laptop; expose the MCP server publicly via a tunnel so claude.ai can read and write through it; REST API stays bound to 127.0.0.1; auth via identity-provider gating at the tunnel edge plus a shared-secret header validated by the MCP HTTP transport. The desktop UI, Claude Code, and Claude.ai all read and write the same single SQLite file via the same REST API; Claude.ai's path is just MCP-over-tunnel-to-localhost-API instead of MCP-over-stdio-to-localhost-API.
decisions.rationale: Solves the stated problem (claude.ai's data drift) with minimum architectural change. Preserves the desktop UI's local-first behavior — no network latency on CRUD, no offline-mode design needed. The migration to remote-canonical (Option B) is left as a separable later workstream if and when the laptop-online-time bound starts hurting; a few months of operating under Option A will reveal what the real claude.ai read/write patterns are and inform whether the larger migration is justified.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Option B: remote-canonical — move the SQLite to a small VPS (DigitalOcean, Hetzner, Fly.io) or migrate to a managed DB (Postgres, Turso). Always-on access from any actor. Costs $5-15/mo. Requires the desktop UI to handle network latency on every read and write, and requires an offline story that does not exist today; the v2 access layer assumes local SQLite. Apply scripts would re-target the remote API (mostly a config change). The async DB-access patterns and connection-pool management add real implementation work. Rejected as premature — the gain (always-on claude.ai access) does not yet justify the desktop-side rework. Option C: hybrid scheduled push of read-only snapshot to cloud — keep canonical DB local, push a daily or per-commit snapshot to a read-only cloud endpoint claude.ai polls. Cheapest and simplest. Rejected because it explicitly does not solve the stated problem: claude.ai would still write out-of-date prompts because the snapshot it reads is itself out of date. The asymmetry between session-time writes and stale reads is precisely the failure mode the user is trying to escape.
decisions.consequences: Claude.ai's database access is bounded by laptop online time. When the laptop is asleep or off-network, claude.ai's MCP calls return connection errors and the conversation falls back to whatever it can infer from conversation memory — current behavior, just in the gaps rather than everywhere. The shared-secret header lives in claude.ai's MCP server configuration (custom headers) and on Doug's laptop in an environment variable or config file; rotation is a manual step. Operational footprint adds one persistent service (cloudflared as a launchd job). The MCP server code needs a transport flag and an auth middleware — both small additions, scoped under PI-045. Option B remains available as a future workstream; the apply scripts, MCP tool registrations, and desktop access patterns all stay compatible because the wire format (FastAPI REST + MCP protocol) does not change between Option A and a future Option B.

decisions.title: Public exposure scope: MCP server only, REST API stays on localhost
decisions.context: DEC-201 commits to exposing the v2 stack to claude.ai through a tunnel. The follow-on question is what gets tunneled. The candidates are (a) MCP server only, with the MCP process speaking to the API over 127.0.0.1 unchanged, or (b) both the MCP server and the REST API, which would let Claude Code prompts and ad-hoc curl run remotely as well.
decisions.decision: Expose the MCP server only. The REST API keeps its 127.0.0.1 bind and is reachable only from processes on Doug's laptop. The MCP process speaks to the API over localhost unchanged.
decisions.rationale: Narrows the public surface to the MCP protocol contract (44 named tools with declared schemas) instead of the full FastAPI route table (every endpoint, every list query, every error envelope). The MCP layer also provides natural rate-limiting and tool-selection discipline through the tool-call mechanism that bare HTTP would not. Doug currently runs Claude Code only at the laptop, so the marginal value of exposing the REST API (cross-machine Claude Code runs) does not justify the doubled attack surface and doubled auth-policy maintenance.
decisions.alternatives_considered: (a) Expose both MCP and REST API — appealing because Claude Code prompts could run from any machine and apply scripts could target the remote API directly. Rejected because Doug's actual Claude Code usage is laptop-local, and the security posture cost is real. (b) Expose REST API only and have claude.ai talk REST through a thin shim — rejected because claude.ai's MCP integration is the supported protocol and the existing MCP tool definitions already provide the right abstraction layer.
decisions.consequences: Cloudflare Tunnel ingress has exactly one route (mcp.crmbuilder.com → 127.0.0.1:<mcp_port>). Cloudflare Access has exactly one policy. If a future use case for remote REST API access surfaces (cross-machine apply scripts, remote Claude Code), exposing it is a separate decision and a separate tunnel route — explicitly not bundled here. The MCP server's HTTP transport binds to 127.0.0.1 (so it is reachable from cloudflared but not from anywhere else on the network); cloudflared dials it as an internal connection.

decisions.title: Engagement routing for the remote MCP: marker-driven single deployment versus per-engagement tunnel deploys
decisions.context: The v2 API is single-engagement per process — cli.py:run_api reads current_engagement.json (written by the desktop UI's Engagements panel) or takes --engagement on launch to pick which engagement's SQLite database it serves. CRMBUILDER (dogfood) and CBM (paper-test client) are separate databases. When claude.ai opens a conversation, it needs to read the right one. The routing question is whether to run one remote MCP that follows whichever engagement is currently active in current_engagement.json (matching how the local desktop API works) or run one remote MCP per engagement (separate tunnel, separate API process, separate claude.ai MCP URL for CRMBUILDER versus CBM).
decisions.decision: Single remote MCP, marker-driven. The deployed API + MCP read current_engagement.json the same way the local stack does. When Doug switches engagements in the desktop UI's Engagements panel, the remote MCP's next request serves the new engagement. Claude.ai's MCP integration is registered once with one URL (mcp.crmbuilder.com).
decisions.rationale: Matches the existing single-engagement-active workflow with zero new state for Doug to manage — engagement switching already happens in the desktop UI, and the remote MCP becomes another consumer of that switch. Operational footprint stays at one tunnel, one API process, one MCP process, one Access policy. Doug's pattern of working on one engagement at a time across all surfaces (desktop, Claude Code, Claude.ai) means the simpler model fits actual usage.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Per-engagement deploys — run one tunnel + API + MCP triple per engagement (mcp-crmbuilder.crmbuilder.com, mcp-cbm.crmbuilder.com), let claude.ai pick by registering both URLs in its MCP integration and choosing which one to call. Architecturally cleaner because the engagement is in the URL path and there is no implicit state in current_engagement.json. Rejected because it roughly doubles the operational footprint (two cloudflared services, two API processes, two Access policies, two MCP URLs in claude.ai), and Doug switches engagements rarely enough that marker discipline is not burdensome.
decisions.consequences: Known footgun: if Doug switches engagements in the desktop UI mid-conversation (rare but possible — e.g., reaches a stopping point on CBM and flips back to CRMBUILDER while a claude.ai window is still open), claude.ai's reads silently switch to the new engagement. The API process needs either to re-read current_engagement.json on each request (cheap, ~µs file stat) or to detect a marker change and restart itself. Mitigation is in PI-045's scope. The current API behavior of reading the marker once at launch needs to change for the remote deployment to be safe under engagement switching. If the per-engagement model becomes more attractive later (e.g., regular concurrent multi-engagement work), the migration cost is one extra cloudflared tunnel and one extra API process — not architectural.

decisions.title: Tunnel product and host: Cloudflare Tunnel on mcp.crmbuilder.com with Cloudflare Access identity gating
decisions.context: DEC-201 and DEC-202 commit to publicly exposing the MCP server. The implementation choices are tunnel product (Cloudflare Tunnel, Tailscale Funnel, ngrok, raw SSH reverse tunnel) and host (which domain and subdomain). Doug confirmed during the conversation that crmbuilder.com is the right host — it is the project-scoped domain Doug already owns, and the project-scoped framing is clearer than using dougbower.com (his personal domain). The domain is not currently on Cloudflare.
decisions.decision: Cloudflare Tunnel on subdomain mcp.crmbuilder.com. Cloudflare Access policy gates by identity (Google login) before requests reach the MCP. A shared-secret header (X-CRMBuilder-Secret or similar) is validated by the MCP HTTP transport as a second-layer check in case Access is misconfigured. Prerequisite operational step: move crmbuilder.com nameservers from the current registrar to Cloudflare (free plan, ~15 minutes).
decisions.rationale: Cloudflare Tunnel gives a stable hostname free, supports Cloudflare Access identity gating free, and ties cleanly to a domain Doug already owns. The two-layer auth (Access at the edge + shared secret at the MCP) means a misconfiguration at either layer alone does not expose the write surface to the internet. The project-scoped subdomain reads correctly in claude.ai's MCP integration listing and in any future documentation.
decisions.alternatives_considered: Tailscale Funnel — no DNS migration needed and Tailscale's ACLs are clean, but ties identity to tailnet membership rather than Google login (cuts off claude.ai web sessions that are not authenticated via tailnet); also gives a {machine}.{tailnet}.ts.net hostname rather than a project-scoped one. Rejected on the auth-model fit. ngrok — free tier has unstable hostnames that change on each restart; paid tier (~$8/mo) for a reserved subdomain; auth via reserved-domain restriction is weaker than Cloudflare Access. Rejected on cost and auth. Raw SSH reverse tunnel from a small VPS — no identity gating at all without additional layers; rejected on auth.
decisions.consequences: crmbuilder.com domain administration moves to Cloudflare's DNS console (the domain registrar is unchanged; just the nameservers point to Cloudflare). cloudflared installs as a launchd service on Doug's laptop and reconnects automatically on reboot or network change. The shared secret lives in claude.ai's MCP server custom-header configuration and in an environment variable Doug's local MCP process reads; rotation is a manual edit in both places. If Doug later wants to expose other services under crmbuilder.com (a status page, a docs site), the domain is already on Cloudflare and adding ingress is straightforward.

planning_items.title: V2 remote-access deployment: expose MCP server publicly so claude.ai can read and write the v2 governance database
planning_items.description: Implement the four decisions from SES-064 (DEC-201 local-canonical with tunneled MCP; DEC-202 MCP-only exposure; DEC-203 marker-driven single deployment; DEC-204 Cloudflare Tunnel on mcp.crmbuilder.com with Access + shared-secret auth). Scope: (a) Code changes delivered as CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md files: add --transport {stdio,streamable-http} flag to the crmbuilder-v2-mcp CLI entry point in crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/cli.py, parameterize the transport in mcp_server/server.py main(), bind the FastMCP HTTP transport to 127.0.0.1 with a configurable port, add a shared-secret header middleware to the HTTP transport that 401s missing or wrong secrets, address the engagement-marker-mid-process risk identified in DEC-203 by either re-reading current_engagement.json per request or failing loud on marker change with restart-required signal. (b) Operational steps: move crmbuilder.com nameservers from current registrar to Cloudflare (free plan); install cloudflared on Doug's laptop and configure as a launchd service; create the named tunnel and DNS record routing mcp.crmbuilder.com to 127.0.0.1:<mcp_port>; create the Cloudflare Access policy requiring Google login (Doug's identity) for the mcp.crmbuilder.com hostname; register the MCP URL in claude.ai's MCP integration with the shared-secret custom header. (c) Verification: smoke-test all 44 MCP tools end-to-end from a claude.ai conversation against both CRMBUILDER and CBM engagements (flip the engagement marker between runs); confirm an unauthenticated request from a non-Doug browser is blocked at the Access layer; confirm a request with wrong shared secret is blocked at the MCP layer; confirm engagement marker drives the right database. (d) Documentation: README section in crmbuilder-v2/ covering remote deployment setup; entry in repo CLAUDE.md under the v2 section describing the remote MCP URL and auth model; the deployment kickoff prompt itself (the in-flight follow-on conversation deliverable, drafted before any of the above starts).
